sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
And then we came back to Krakow, and my poor mother there, stayed there.
But before they close the Warsaw ghetto-- I don't remember exactly how it happened-- but she left.
And she went to the city of Tarnow.
Apparently, you know, there were deportations.
You know, the Germans, it was like moving chess on the chess board, you know?
And she went to Tarnow.
She was there in the ghetto.
And she, again, she opened her practice.
I think it was 1941.
We went-- we had to.
We had to.
And we had-- everybody was assigned an apartment.
And at the beginning, the living conditions were not that terrible yet.
Later, they started to deport people, like they did in Warsaw, from all the surrounding villages, and cities, and towns.
So then it will-- became very bad.
We got a one bedroom apartment with a kitchen with another couple and their daughter.
And I said to my husband, why should we share?
If we stay together in-- in, you know, in the one bedroom, we have to share the kitchen.
I said, how about if we get the kitchen?
We slept in the kitchen.
We didn't mind.
And it was OK.
And this is how it was.
But some other families were even worse than-- terribly crowded as the time progressed.
I was assigned work in a factory, which in those days, repair the German uniforms.
You know, they send the uniforms from the front.
Very often they were bloody.
Very often they had lice.
And all we had to do is sew by hand.
I didn't know how to sew on the machine.
You know, the buttons and sometimes something was torn, so we fixed it.
This is how we did.
And in the meantime, the-- I think at the very beginning of the ghetto-- you know, my memory is not so hot either now anymore.
We used to go out to that factory every day.
And we were being brought back by-- by the Germans-- by the SS-- back to the ghetto.
But later, we didn't go out anymore.
We worked within the ghetto, inside.
Yes.
We--
Terrible, because-- first of all, you know, I'm not talking already about the food and the living conditions.
One doesn't know how one can get acclimated to certain situation.
But the fear of deportations-- because they arranged every few months when you didn't expect it-- you expected the-- the least, you know.
And-- and-- the-- they-- they told you to gather on a-- on a square.
You know, in one part of-- part of the ghetto was a special place.
And they then they did to the right and to the left, whoever they please.
Mostly, older people and children were taken, in-- even in those early days of the ghetto.
And only the people with the work permit could stay.
In those days, we didn't, but there were starting to be rumors.

Very soon, about early 1942, when the deportations were more frequent-- and I understand that some people were able-- like one man they were talking about was able to flee, to escape from Belzec, you know.
And he was telling the stories.
Belzec was like Majdanek, like Treblinka.
It was a death camp.
It was not a work camp or, you know, where you could work.
This was specifically a death camp.
So-- but nobody believed this man.
You know what I mean?
He said that people are being exterminated there in the-- I don't think they had a-- a crematoria yet, but in the gas chambers.
Nobody believed him.
You know, we heard rumors, you know?
But we didn't believe it, because, you see, God forbid, it would happen now.
We had a precedent in history.
We would be maybe watchful.
Maybe we will be more frightened.
But in those days, there was no precedent in history.
Who could visualize that a man is capable to do something like this to another man?
Poldek was working.
Do I remember?
Poldek was working-- where was he working at that time?
You know, I don't remember.
He will tell you himself, because I don't remember.
Because he-- in the camp, he did several things, and I don't remember exactly.
Well, this was not the food that the Germans-- this was not the German allowance.
Everybody tried to buy something on a black market.
We were, you know, this was in the city of Krakow.
The ghetto's walls were closed.
But a lot of Polish people were coming and selling by the-- you know, somehow the food was getting into the ghetto.
Maybe the police brought it in.
Maybe-- I don't know who.
The Germans and the Polish police.
Polish police was mostly like the helpers of the German police.
No.
Only when I was working, I was going with a group.
I never went.
Poldek-- I know now what Poldek was doing-- now-- now.
Yeah.
This was at the beginning of the ghetto when I think that-- that you could get out.
Yeah, the ghetto wasn't closed.
And he had his bicycle.
And he also had to sell certain things on the black market, you know?
And-- and this is how we lived.
This is how everybody lived.
This was no way-- no other way of-- because for our work, we were not getting paid.
[LAUGHS]
I am sure that the-- the-- the Jews who were religious-- I think I became a much better Jew during the war than I ever was before the war.
Because first of all, I was very young.
So when you're young, you don't-- and I knew I was Jewish.
And I was proud of it.
And always-- and we had the Jewish holidays we celebrated, with my mother's aunt and uncle.
But as-- as far as-- otherwise, I-- but in the ghetto, I learned more about the Jewish holy days and how to, you know, about the religious aspects of everything.
I-- I learned much more.
No.
There were no synagogues, no.
No.
No.
Some of them they burned.
Some of them they burned with people inside, which were-- happened many, many times.
No, no.
We couldn't go.
Well, I don't know what you call atrocities.
But when you go to work, and you come after work to the ghetto, and you see the ghetto 1/2 empty, you know that something terrible has-- has happened, you know.
And these people never came back.
These people never let anybody know.
There was always a lot of shooting going on during the deportations, you know.
We were-- later on, we were both working in the-- what's this called-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a German place of work.
It was like a German rest home for the soldiers coming from the war, kind of from the hospitals, before they went home or before they went again to the front.
And we were going on big trucks.
This was later on.
They were picking us up, and we went out of the ghetto.
And one day, they were very nice, I must say.
This was the army-- the Wehrmacht-- the German army.
And they were, you know, they were kind of decent to us.
And one day they said to us, you're not going back to the ghetto today.
We need you.
They didn't say why.
And they told us to stay.
They gave us something to eat.
They gave us-- told us to sleep on the tables under the benches.
The next day, we worked all day.
And in the evening, as usual, they took us back to the ghetto.
And the ghetto was 1/2 empty.
Poldek's entire family, you know, his aunts, his cousins-- he had a big family-- everybody was gone.
So this was-- and during the deportations, they were shooting.
They shot people in the hospitals.
They shoot-- shot people on the street.
And then when the deportation was almost over, they were going with dogs, and with God knows what else, looking for people who were hiding.
1942 mostly.
Oh, we knew it.
People where we-- we came back to the ghetto.
And they told us this one was shot today, or this one's baby was shot, or this happened, or that happened.
See, those things-- I was-- we were lucky enough-- I was lucky enough-- that I never saw anybody being shot in front of my eyes, you know?
But I knew when I came back from work, and I hear this one was taken, and this one was shot, and this baby was killed.
So that was enough to know.
Well, they always did it, you know.
As soon as deportation was finished, they were bringing some other people.
Well, then they liquidated the ghetto, which was-- this was the most bloody thing, you know.
And they-- they were absolutely ruthless in those couple days which they did it.
And we were supposed to go to the concentration camp of Plaszow.
Plaszow was not far away from Krakow.
And we walked to--
P-L-- well, in Polish, you have an L-- L--
--with a thing.
But in English, P-L-A-S-Z-O-W.
Plaszow was very close.
We walked there, so probably walked about 1/2 an hour with our bundles.
This was the SS, of course.
And already in the ghetto, during the liquidation, the future commandant of the Camp Plaszow was very active there, killing people, and then-- to the right and to the left.
And it was a purely German operation.
I was-- I tell you, I was a very lucky person.
Because I personally never-- I heard a lot of shooting.
We knew.
There was a hill.
In those days, our camp, Plaszow, didn't have a gas chamber.
And they used to bring people either from our camp or from outlying areas, people caught on Aryan papers, you know, people who had false papers, Jews who were caught on Aryan papers.
There was a hill, and they were bringing the people to that hill and shooting them.
So that we heard in the camp all day long-- all day long.
And then they were burning the bodies all around.
We saw them when they were digging out the bodies, you see, when they were-- in 19-- I think, 44.
No, no.
I-- my husband told me.
He had very good friends-- Professor Alexandro [NON-ENGLISH], his wife and their son-- and they were supposed-- they had plans to escape through the sewers.
And my husband thought we'll be able to do the same.
And he--
Sewers were leading to the Aryan section outside of the ghetto.
And so my husband left.
And the German megaphones were announcing constantly, get out of the apartments, because if you get caught, you will be shot.
And we will be looking, and this and that.
And I was left all alone.
Almost-- no, I mean, that big, big apartment house, everybody left, going to Plaszow.
But my husband told me to wait.
So I waited.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then he came.
Then I realized I was so alone over there.
I got scared.
And I walked out.
I took a little bundle with me.
And I-- and I think I joined one of the very last groups from the ghetto going toward the camp.
And my husband, in the meantime, came to this empty apartment.
He didn't see me.
And he started to look for me.
And then in the evening, he also came with the-- with less groups of-- of people.
Oh, thank you.
Thanks.
I always get hoarse when I get nervous.
Yes.
And my husband, for a while, was a Jewish police also, at the very, very beginning of the ghetto.
Well, they thought-- the policemen who were recruited for that purpose-- thought this will be in order to keep some kind of order within the ghetto.
The people should get to work, and from work, and in distribution of the food, and in the-- you know.
And-- but then it became very evident that when the deportations started that they were using-- were using the Polish-- I mean the Jewish police for deportations.
And this is when some people, and among them my husband, started to fake.
He went to his favorite doctor, Dr.
Biberstein, in the ghetto, and he explained to him that he doesn't want-- he didn't want to be in it anymore.
Because the commandant of the Jewish police was a very common, very, really horrible human being.
And he didn't want to let these people who wanted to leave the police to go.
So they had to--
Yeah, and how-- yeah.
So they needed a legitimate excuse.
So Dr.
Biberstein suggested to my husband to fake a backache.
My husband always had a little problem with his backache.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- that was how it was called, yeah.
Then we came to Plaszow.
He was the same day.
He came back.
He came, and he found me.
It was not that difficult.
You know, all people from Krakow knew each other, you know.
And he found the woman barracks.
He found me, but of course, we were separated.
He was-- well, the woman barracks were like up on the hill a little bit, and he was down in the men's barracks, yeah.
Well, it was a terrible life.
Because we had to sleep on the bunks, a few of us.
And it was not as bad as Auschwitz where we slept 13 to a bunk.
But it was like three to a bunk.
You know, up-- two upstairs and two downstairs, you know, lower bunks and upper bunk.
We never had enough food.
We worked hard.
We had night shifts.
And, again, the same thing-- constantly shootings, deportations.
You never knew when our camp commandant or one of his other SS men would come and shoot people for-- for the smallest, smallest-- for nothing, really.
I was working again.
First, I think from Plaszow also, we used to go out to work in that factory-- and then-- oh, I was working for the factory of Madrich.
Also this was not the uniforms, but we were making overalls for German workers.
And he was a pretty good boss.
But he could not-- you know, he didn't have the-- the right to intervene on our behalf or anything.
So if the Germans felt like coming during the night shift and shoot a few people, they couldn't say anything, you know.
He helped us with a little bit extra bread.
That's how much I remember.
And I remember that he personally never shot anybody, never persecuted.
He was rather nice.
He demanded work, and he got it.
And he was rather decent--
--to us.
But he-- I don't remember him helping in any other way except maybe some extra bread.
I heard about Schindler from the very beginning of the war.
Yes.
Because when I came to Krakow-- you know, I don't remember whether Schindler came to my-- must have been when I was there already.
Schindler came to my mother in law's apartment.
I wasn't there.
Or maybe this was before.
You know, I don't remember.
Maybe this was because-- yeah.
This must have been before--
--because I would have remembered it better.
He was-- this was when he was still hiding--
--kind of.
You know?
And he and his mother were alone in the house.
This must have been before we lived with them--
--and maybe even before we came.
And--
I-- when I met, you know, his family, because his entire family was working really for him-- specifically for him.
Because Schindler wanted my mother-in-law to help him with certain things in the house.
Everything in Poland had to be made to order, you know?
She made-- and when he met Poldek, he also realized-- Mr.
Schindler-- that Poldek would be his liaison between other people.
You know, he needed things.
You couldn't go to the store in those days.
The stores were empty.
And you had to have-- even the Germans couldn't get everything.
But our Jewish people had to get rid of their things.
If they wanted to survive, they had to sell them-- jewelry, furs-- at the beginning, before we gave away the furs.
Because the German collected the furs later on-- Persian rugs, everything.
If you wanted shirts, they had to be made to order.
If you wanted everything.
There-- no, with the camp, this was nothing anymore.
He couldn't get out from the ghetto.
Poldek was--
--still going on the bicycle.
I think that my husband used to-- he used to work in the garage, my husband.
So there, he used to meet sometimes Schindler.
But, you know, Schindler was in another place outside of Plaszow, in his factory.
And so I suppose, you know, he didn't see him very often.
Oh, yes.
When I came, it was already almost built--
--you know, absolutely.
Because this was the site of the previous Jewish cemetery.
This was the perfidy of Germans, yeah.
Well, this-- they-- they were the-- monuments, the stones, taken and the roads were made out of them.
And this was a terrible feeling to walk over something like this, seeing the names, the dates, you know.
And-- and-- and it was a terrible feeling.
But all of us were so preoccupied with our survival that this was the least that we could worry about, you know?
Oh, all the time, all the time.
On his horse, without his horse, he was the person who was most terribly feared by everybody, you know.

We heard-- you know, when we came, at the time when I was still working in Plaszow, but going to work out-- I mean going to work outside of Plaszow, we used to come when it was already dark, in the winter days.
And we were being control by the gates, you know, to the camp.
Well, we-- we had-- we had the lice.
We had started to have the lice, especially in the summer months, you know?
We also had bed--
19-- 1943 we came to the camp, right?
So it was '43, '44 until we left.
And also, the terrible plague was bed bugs.
We slept on the bunks, and in the warm summer months, you know, it was so terribly hot.
It was like a shower of bed bugs.
They used to go in your ears, in your noses.
And I never forget the day--
Bed bugs are when the house is not very clean.
I don't think in America-- [LAUGHS] --they exist.
I don't know.
It's like-- I think it's a little reddish-- small and reddish in color, brownish-reddish.
Horrible.
They bite you.
They bite you.
And they used to come down from-- I don't know-- from the ceiling.
We used to have it in our noses and in our ears.
And I never forget that that part-- one particular night we couldn't stand it anymore.
So we decided-- in front of our barracks was like some ground, grassy ground, you know?
So some of us decided to go and sleep in front of the barrack.
In the meantime, the Germans came, a few of them, and they started to beat the girls horribly, because they went outside, you know.
It was a terrible time.
We also had to all wash in the latrines, you know.
Everything was better than we-- we had afterwards, in Auschwitz.
But it was from horrible as comparing--
--to our regular life.
No, no, no-- only for women.
Men and-- or women.
You know, it was not allowed.
Some men did.
Mostly not men and not husbands and wives-- [LAUGHS] --but, you know, boyfriends, girlfriends.
Sometimes it happens, but this was severely punished too.
A man could have been shot like if he will be caught.
Hanging, yes.
Hanging, yes, and I also saw people shot--
Well, because this was an instance when a few prisoners tried to escape.
So in order to teach the rest of the prisoners a lesson, they arranged for all of us to stay on the appellplatz, which was a roll-call square, and witness the execution.
And they were hanged.
And the whole-- the whole camp had to be there and witness this.

On another occasion, the Germans killed the-- the Polish-- the Jewish Police commandant-- the one I mentioned before-- his assistant, the commandant's wife, and a few other people, connected with the Jewish police.
And they were-- I didn't see the shooting, but I saw them dead, laying on the ground.
And the whole camp had to pass by them and look at them.
